Article.ArticleTitle: Online monitoring of deposit-induced corrosion in a Waste-to-Energy boiler.

Abstract.AbstractText: Heat-transfer surfaces in waste-to-energy boilers are exposed to chlorine-rich ash deposits, which can cause severe and rapidly evolving corrosion and make timely condition assessment challenging. An online monitoring system was employed to monitor corrosion by integrating Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR). A 31-day field test was carried out above the grate in the combustion zone, yielding real-time corrosion-rate data. The results reveal three stages in the corrosion-deposition evolution of TP347H: pronounced initial corrosion, intermediate suppression associated with deposit buildup, and a late stage characterized by relatively stable deposition behavior. The corrosion rate is sensitive to variations in flue-gas temperature. Cumulative metal loss obtained by integrating LPR-derived corrosion rates is comparable to cross-sectional measurements and slightly underestimates the measured loss, with a relative error of 28.2%, supporting the practical applicability of the proposed method under high-temperature, chlorine-rich conditions. Ash chemistry and corrosion-scale microscopy further suggest a dual role of the deposit layer: it provides a short-term thermal-resistance barrier while serving as a long-term reservoir for chlorine species.
